We've already discussed the power of loss aversion, the very human tendency to avoid the loss of something more than to acquire the gain of that same thing. In fact, in research by Nobel Prize winner Daniel Kahneman, the tendency to avoid losses was twice as strong as that for obtaining gains. So how can we harness the force of loss aversion in our influence appeal? One way involves the use of loss framing, in which we frame the reasons for selecting our offer, not primarily in terms of the benefits and advantages that will be gained if prospects move in our direction, but rather primarily in terms of the benefits and advantages they will miss or forego if they don't choose us. Note that I'm not suggesting that we never mention gains that people can acquire from our products and services, it's just that to arrange for others to act, to stop dithering and get off the fence, we should be sure to let them know what they'll lose if they don't. That way we can still sell to benefits, but we should emphasize that they're benefits that are not to be missed.
